Hi, I'm Alberto, I know a little bit of coding but it takes me a lot of time to code something, I had an idea to improve my learning English method using subtitles.

I use to watch series and films in English to learn. In my actual point for me is very easy to watch a movie with English subtitles but hard to watch it without subtitles. I got and idea to make an intermediate point between those two methods.

We can make a simple software to spoil the .srt (subtitles file), the software change random words to a lines rows.

For example:

1 00:02:12,087 --> 00:02:13,964 - Jump! - What are you doing?

2 00:02:24,727 --> 00:02:26,718 - Oh, my God! - What are you doing?

3 00:02:28,527 --> 00:02:29,721 Help me!

1 00:02:12,087 --> 00:02:13,964 - Jump! - What ___ you doing?

2 00:02:24,727 --> 00:02:26,718 - Oh, __ God! - What ___ you _____?

We can set in the code a rate like (0.55) the code is going to point every word in the text avoiding the numbers and the arrow "-->", after pointing a word the code is going to cast a random function to change the word for a lines row like: "What" for "____".

I ran into an issue where the header included `windows.h`.  I've read #42, and I understand why you thought it was a good idea to keep it, but one thing that would help a whole bunch is defining `NOMINMAX` before including `windows.h`.  This specifically is a major pain-point when including `windows.h`, as the min and max macros conflict with `std::min` and `std::max`, which seems like behavior you would want to avoid in most cases.

For what it's worth, `SDL_opengl.h` appears to do exactly what I describe.

Given `/home/user/project/.vintrc.yaml` and `/home/user/.vintrc.yaml`, `ConfigProjectSource` will first find the project config, but then also the user's global config, which it then uses instead.

I think a sensible fix for this is to stop once the first config has been found.

Also, ignoring the global config explicitly might make sense.
Vint could stop at the user's home directory to address this?!

Also, `ConfigGlobalSource` should look at `~/.config/vint/config` (`$XDG_CONFIG_HOME/yamllint/config`) first, which would work around this.

issue_title: The Other Async, waiting for wait_for
issue_body:
Hi David! (et. al)

I was watching your PyGotham talk, "The Other Async" and I was looking that the diabolical crazyness of the universal queue thingy, and suddenly I went hmmmm.. why.. are you awaiting asyncio.wait_for?

Asyncio already knows how to await a wrapped future, so unless I am missing something doing `await wait_for(f, None)` does nothing for you. Basically there is no point in using wait_for unless you provide a timeout.

Example:


    import asyncio
    from concurrent.futures import Future
    import time
    from threading import Thread

    f = Future()

    def set_delayed(item, delay):
        time.sleep(delay)
        f.set_result(item)

    Thread(target=lambda:set_delayed('Hi David!', 10)).start()

    async def get_future():
        print(await asyncio.wrap_future(f))

    asyncio.get_event_loop().run_until_complete(get_future())

_From [liulk@google.com](https://code.google.com/u/liulk@google.com/) on November 28, 2012 12:43:09_

Using Python 2.7 which randomizes hash key order, ip.__load_protos() may implicitly __import__() the ip6 module before some of the other protocols are populated into ip.IP._protosw.  If we do IP6._protosw.update(ip.IP._protosw) as is done currently, then IP6 would only get a subset of protocols that happen to be defined so far, and due to hash key randomization, this could very well be the empty set.

Moreover, ip6 could not import ip before class IP6 is defined.  The workaround here is to do this after class IP6, forcing the sharing of dictionary rather than making a copy.

import ip
IP6._protosw = ip.IP._protosw

Attaching a patch which I verified to work.

liulk

**Attachment:** [ip6.py.diff](http://code.google.com/p/dpkt/issues/detail?id=99)

_Original issue: http://code.google.com/p/dpkt/issues/detail?id=99_

issue_title: Should not require pytest-runner to install the library
issue_body:
Listing pytest-runner in the setup_requires metadata means that in order to install keyring, even in production, the test runner is needed. There's no reason to do that, since if pytest-runner is installed it can run the tests just fine without being in setup_requires.

Having this installation dependency is currently breaking the automated tests in several OpenStack projects which are trying to use keyring.

----------------------------------------
- Bitbucket: https://bitbucket.org/kang/python-keyring-lib/issue/106
- Originally reported by: Doug Hellmann
- Originally created at: 2013-07-21T18:45:05.463

issue_title: Callable's arguments' types may be lost
issue_body:
As requested, I am opening this issue instead of #44.

Seen with enforce `0.3.4`.

Example code where a bound method is used:
```python
from typing import Optional, Callable, Any, Dict
import enforce

class Example:
    @enforce.runtime_validation
    def method(self, d: Dict):
        pass

@enforce.runtime_validation
def bound_callback_example(callback: Optional[Callable[[Dict], Any]]=None):
    pass

example = Example()
bound_callback_example(example.method)
```

Funnily two different exceptions may be raised by the above code:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "enforce_examples.py", line 14, in <module>
    bound_callback_example(example.method)
  File ".../venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/enforce/decorators.py", line 104, in universal
    _args, _kwargs, _ = enforcer.validate_inputs(parameters)
  File ".../venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/enforce/enforcers.py", line 86, in validate_inputs
    raise RuntimeTypeError(exception_text)
enforce.exceptions.RuntimeTypeError: 
  The following runtime type errors were encountered:
       Argument 'callback' was not of type typing.Union[typing.Callable[[typing.Dict], typing.Any], NoneType]. Actual type was BoundFunctionWrapper.
```

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "enforce_examples.py", line 14, in <module>
    bound_callback_example(example.method)
  File ".../venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/enforce/decorators.py", line 104, in universal
    _args, _kwargs, _ = enforcer.validate_inputs(parameters)
  File ".../venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/enforce/enforcers.py", line 86, in validate_inputs
    raise RuntimeTypeError(exception_text)
enforce.exceptions.RuntimeTypeError: 
  The following runtime type errors were encountered:
       Argument 'callback' was not of type typing.Union[typing.Callable[[typing.Dict], typing.Any], NoneType]. Actual type was typing.Callable.
```

For now, the problem can be avoided by using a less strict type for the `callback`, to the detriment of self-documenting code and pushing the burden of type verification to the callable itself (if needed):
```python
@enforce.runtime_validation
def bound_callback_example(callback: Optional[Callable]=None):
    pass
```

issue_title: Remove support for escape characters in bewit `ext`
issue_body: In order to [fix header parsing](https://github.com/kumar303/mohawk/issues/34) the `ext` value can no longer contain escape characters. However, this limitation was not imposed on `ext` values used by [bewit](https://github.com/kumar303/mohawk/blob/master/mohawk/bewit.py) (AKA signed URLs). Let's put the same limitations on `ext` values used by the bewit function.

There is problem syncronizing large files , since its done all in memory , eventually you ll run out of it
Download to memory --> flush to disk

I implemented some kind of a streaming solution:

```python
class StreamingFileTarget(FsTarget):
   
    from functools import partial

    def write_file(self, name, source, blocksize=DEFAULT_BLOCKSIZE, callback=None):

        def write_stream_data(fp_dst, callback, data):
            fp_dst.write(data)
            if callback:
                callback(data)

        self.check_write(name)

        with open(os.path.join(self.cur_dir, name), "wb") as fp_dst:
            write_stream_partial = partial(write_stream_data, fp_dst, callback)
            source.read_stream(name, write_stream_partial)

class StreamingFtpTarget(FtpTarget):

    def read_stream(self, name, callback):
        self.ftp.retrbinary("RETR %s" % name, callback)

```

issue_title: netlink attribute payload byteorder issue on bigendian machines
issue_body:
There seems to be some issue with nla messages on big-endian machines. When running a simple script like this (pyroute2 0.3.6):
```python
from pyroute2 import IPRSocket

ip = IPRSocket()
ip.bind()
while 1:
  print "%s" % ip.get()
ip.close()
```
I occasionally get traces like the one below:
```
WARNING:root:decoding NDA_DST
WARNING:root:Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "build/bdist.linux-ppc64/egg/pyroute2/netlink/__init__.py", line 1084, in decode_nlas
    nla.decode()
  File "build/bdist.linux-ppc64/egg/pyroute2/netlink/__init__.py", line 1215, in decode
    family = self.family_map[self.parent['family']]
KeyError: 33554432
```
This happens when fmt in pyrote2.netlink.nlmsg_base.decode() is 'i', I've also looked at raw data in that message and it is `0x02 0x00 0x00 0x00`, so a little-endian 2.
It looks like pyroute2 expects NLAs to be in host's byte-order, but at least according to [this](http://linux-development-for-fresher.blogspot.cz/2012/05/understanding-netlink-socket.html) it is possible for payload to be in network byte-order.

issue_title: Changing a stack should be possible using previous parameter values.
issue_body:
When updating a stack, most of the parameters do not change and it can often be cumbersome to pass in all the same parameters that the previous stack uses, especially if it was randomly generated (e.g. a generated password for a DB instance).

The `aws-cli` provides a `UsePreviousValue` option: something similar would definitely be useful. The straight forward way would probably be to have a flag `--reuse-parameters <Parameter1>,<Parameter2>,<Parameter3>...`.

Thoughts?

issue_title: Exception raised by the logging if there is an encoding problem in sender name
issue_body:
In imbox/parser.py, the decoding of the name and e-mail address is done by ```decode_mail_header()```. It's done 3 times, by ```imbox.utils.str_encode()``` function:
 * line 36: ```logger.debug()``` formats the name of the sender. ```errors``` parameter is not set at the call so it's in strict mode.
 * lines 38 and 42, in order to set ```headers[index]```: Encoding problems are fixed by the ```replace``` value to third parameter of the ```str_decode()``` call ().

I have a case where it's a problem: the content of the name is not correct utf-8 string so I get an ```UnicodeDecodeError``` due to the logger and not the functional code of the library. If I set ```errors='replace'``` for the ```str_encode()``` call of the logger, it works properly.

In my opinion, setting errors parameter to ```replace``` is a good way to fix it. What do you think about it?
I can do a PR with my testcase for unit testing it.

issue_title: Won't check SHA1 when the certificate is not valid
issue_body:
With the update to 0.14.x the SHA1 verification has been moved in the xml. And if the certificate is not valid (expired, self-signed, or untrusted root) the `hasSha1SignedCertificate` tag won't be present. But it will be present if the certificate is valid on a wrong hostname.
I think it will be more consistent for post-analysis if the tag was available in any use case, and even if the certificate is untrusted, the SHA1 info should not be dismissed because it is still relevant to list all of the issues an URL could have.

issue_title: Saved text File has 00 bytes interleaved

issue_body:
So the `wcmatch.glob` and `wcmatch.iglob` work on relative paths. The way it works is that `**` does not match `.` and `..`. This makes sense when crawling files. So with globstar enabled and and restricting dots (these may be enabled by default in the future), we get:

```python
>>> glob.glob('**/../*.md', flags=glob.G|glob.D)
['.\\docs\\..\\README.md', '.\\docs\\src\\markdown\\_snippets\\..\\changelog.md', '.\\docs\\src\\markdown\\_snippets\\..\\index.md', '.\\docs\\src\\markdown\\_snippets\\..\\installation.md', '.\\docs\\src\\markdown\\_snippets\\..\\license.md', '.\\requirements\\..\\README.md', '.\\tests\\..\\README.md', '.\\wcmatch\\..\\README.md']
```

But when applying globmatch, these paths don't match:

```python
>>> glob.globmatch('.\\requirements\\..\\README.md', '**/../*.md', flags=glob.G|glob.D)
False
```

The crux of the issue is that when crawling, `.` and `..` have special meaning. So you want to first globstar, then either do nothing for `.` or get the parent for `..`.  So `**` should absolutely not capture parents and continue on. Because then it would just be treated as "another file". `.` is what should be found after `**`, not part of `**`. 

But matching is different when trying to get the same results.  Using the example: `'.\\requirements\\..\\README.md'`, we are using simple pattern matching.  It must be noted if `**` can't match a hidden file `.hidden`, `**` so it should be safe. `.` is the same path that you already matched, so we know it is good as well.  It can also represent the current directory.  In the context of matching a glob path, restriction of dot is meant to ignore `.hidden` files, not special directory specifiers.

I think `**` should be able to to match specifically `.` and `..`, but not `.hidden`. `*` should be able to match `.` and `..` as well.  At least in `globmatch`. This could be debated for `fnmatch`.

- `globmatch` should probably match separators as `(?:/\.(?=/|$)|/)+` to match cases like `/file////name/././`  These are stupid ways to write file names, but they should match. If this was on the end of a globstar pattern, it would naturally gather `./` paths.
- If a path does not start with a windows drive or slash, `./` should be an optional assumption `(?:\.(?:/\.(?=/|$)|/)+)?`.

Just some thoughts.  I obviously need to think out these cases well enough to construct a regex that isn't unnecessarily ridiculous.  Maybe our fnmatch needs to just match file names, and globmatch just do directories. Or maybe globmatch doesn't need to be a regex.  It might actually be easier to use the same glob logic minus file crawling.

issue_title: Can't get a Google OAuth2 refresh_token
django login_redirect context processor's value needs escaping
issue_body:
The `value` retrieved in `login_redirect` is automatically un-escaped and needs to be re-escaped in order to be used in a querystring.   Currently if the redirect url is something like "/page/?this=that" then the `REDIRECT_QUERYSTRING` will be "next=/page/?this=that" when it should be "next=/page/%3Fthis%3Dthat".

The way to work around this bug is to use `{{ REDIRECT_FIELD_NAME }}={{ REDIRECT_FIELD_VALUE|urlencode }}` in templates.

issue_title: Python API: CSV name doesn't match filename
issue_body:
This error is coming back from lipd.validate(D) **on the first try, directly after** converting an excel file to LiPD file in python. All subsequent readLipd then validate come back passing 

Why? 

Because the excel template is using 1-indexed naming for it's data sheets, while the Utilities, and all other code, uses 0-indexed naming. 

Example: 

Excel Sheets: 
paleo1measurementTable1
chron1measurementTable1

These sheets generate the filenames: 
NWG-SL.Lasher.2017.chron1measurement1.csv for the table chron0measurement0
NWG-SL.Lasher.2017.paleo1measurement1.csv for the table paleo0measurement0


Why is it only happening when trying to validate directly after converting an excel file? 

Because filenames and table names are not permanent. They are rewritten (to adhere to standard naming) every time you use writeLipd. excel() has a few major steps 
1. convert the excel to and write the LiPD file
2. readLipd file into memory (the one created in step 1)
3. writeLipd the LiPD data in memory back the disk (to save all the inferred data and file standardization corrections)
The csv filenames in memory from step 2 are mismatched, and this is what goes to the validator. The filenames saved to file, in step 3, are corrected for next time.

    def ecb_read(ecb_request):

issue_title: Document the process method required for Service initialization.

issue_body:
Using `forms_valid()` and never calling `form_valid()` means that the `SuccessMessageMixin` can't set any success messages. (`form_valid()` is where `SuccessMessageMixin` injects the messages)

To get round this, I made a new `InlineSuccessMessageMixin`

```python
from django.contrib import messages


class InlineSuccessMessageMixin(object):
    """
    Adds a success message on successful form submission.
    """
    success_message = ''

    def forms_valid(self, form, formset):
        print "something"
        response = super(InlineSuccessMessageMixin, self).forms_valid(form, formset)
        success_message = self.get_success_message(form.cleaned_data)
        if success_message:
            messages.success(self.request, success_message)
        return response

    def get_success_message(self, cleaned_data):
        return self.success_message % cleaned_data
```

It's basically just a copy of the django's built in one.

If you were to implement this, things to note:

It doesn't pass the formset forms' `cleaned_data` to the success message

issue_title: Addons installation freezed because of database lock
issue_body:
This bug append if we run anthem commands in pre: in migrations.yml

Anthem will, on first command, create the database if not exists and installing some base modules by starting an odoo in Context.
This odoo first creation will, inter alia, create the table ir_module_module.

After that, for addons installations setp, marabunta will perform a select on ir_module_module table ([in this method](https://github.com/camptocamp/marabunta/blob/master/marabunta/database.py#L153)) to list already installed module then will perform installations!

But, in case of a module installation try to alter ir_module_module table (e.g. :base_import_module which is installed by web_studio in V10 :)), the previous select will block this ALTER TABLE

issue_title: Wrong table/field names used in query construction
issue_body:
While working on pull request #408, I've noticed that resolving the backend name of fields and tables is either brittle (lots and lots of duplicate code building the name from multiple object attributes) or already broken (`table.sqlsafe` returns the correct backend name only when `table._rname` is set or the table is not aliased, otherwise it'll return the alias which may either not exist in the database or refer to a completely different table).

I've introduced a new property `Table.real_name` in PR #408 that always returns the correct backend name of the table. However, fixing all instances of this problem is beyond the scope of that pull request so I'm reporting this here for later. `Field` doesn't have any property returning the correct full backend name/internal colname either because the `sqlsafe` property relies on broken `Table.sqlsafe`. Whether to fix the existing properties or introduce new ones is open for debate.

issue_body:
**Description**
I came from #10401 
In that issue, there is no reproducible code, so not sure whats the use case there.
When I was developing mxnet backend for keras, I have similar issue.

So I used this [unit test](https://github.com/keras-team/keras/blob/6489c457d03e5fd5d605ed6197932f3b769ff951/tests/keras/layers/normalization_test.py#L144) and was able to reproduce:

**Config**

mxnet: latest master (pip installed mxnet --pre)
keras: 2.2.0 with TensorFlow backend

**The purpose of the test:**
Create a random normalized data with size  size=(1000, 3, 4, 4), centered on 5.0, variance 10.0.
Use batchnorm layer to normalize it to have mean close to 0, and std close to 1.

**Reproducible  code:**
First part of the code shows Keras implementation with TensorFlow backend, you can see mean is close to 0 and std is close to 1. 
Second Part of the code shows Gluon implementation, When scale and center is False, it throws error
```
import mxnet as mx
import numpy as np
from keras.layers import normalization
from keras.models import Sequential
from mxnet import autograd
from mxnet import gluon

Keras test

norm = normalization.BatchNormalization(center=False, scale=False,
                                        input_shape=(3, 4, 4))

model.compile(loss='mse', optimizer='sgd')

# centered on 5.0, variance 10.0

assert_allclose(np.std(out, axis=(0, 2, 3)), 1.0, atol=1e-1)

Gluon test

print("Results from gluon:")
print( "Mean: %s" % np.mean(out, axis=(0, 2, 3)))
print( "Std: %s" % np.std(out, axis=(0, 2, 3)))
assert_allclose(np.mean(out, axis=(0, 2, 3)), 0.0, atol=1e-1)

issue_title: why MKL and MKL-DNN can't be used simultaneously in ChooseBlas.cmake

issue_body:
I am having a performance scaling issue in CPU context. From what I can tell, it looks like this issue could suggest a bottleneck in the call NDArray.asnumpy(). I initially noticed this problem using the old FeedForward class, and more recently duplicated it using the newer Module class; all details below are using Module.

When switching MXNET_CPU_WORKER_NTHREADS and OMP_NUM_THREADS from 1 to 4 on a 4-core machine, I only get a 13% training speed improvement. My CPU usage does increase about threefold, so the settings would seem to be working. Also, I get a 268% speed improvement in a numpy .dot operation, so I do see improvements there.

Given that multithreading seems to be working but there isn't much performance improvement, I tried using cProfile to identify any potential bottlenecks. Using a test function (code below), here are the most expensive calls:

    ncalls  tottime  percall  cumtime  percall filename:lineno(function)
         1    0.011    0.011   11.299   11.299 <string>:1(<module>)
         1    0.000    0.000   10.997   10.997 base_module.py:287(fit)
        21    0.000    0.000   10.752    0.512 module.py:521(update_metric)
        21    0.000    0.000   10.752    0.512 executor_group.py:413(update_metric)
        21    0.002    0.000   10.751    0.512 metric.py:134(update)
        42   10.745    0.256   10.748    0.256 ndarray.py:488(asnumpy)

So, it looks like the program is spending almost all of its time in the two calls to asnumpy() made in the metric's update function. Can anyone explain this effect?

Compiler: Visual Studio 2015
Package used (Python/R/Scala/Julia): Python

issue_title: Examples for --set and --extract do not work as described
issue_body:
After having set up sops and gpg to have a play with sops, I find that the examples for the --set and --extract flags do not work as they stand.

**For `--extract`, the examples in README.rst are:**

`sops -d ~/git/svc/sops/example.yaml --extract '["app2"]["key"]'`
and
`sops -d ~/git/svc/sops/example.yaml --extract '["an_array"][1]'`

For me, neither of these will print only the desired key, but rather the whole file is decrypted and printed to stdout.  I did get it to work in a different parameter order though, like so the single key value is output.

`sops -d --extract '["app2"]["key"]' ~/git/svc/sops/example.yaml`
and
`sops -d --extract '["an_array"][1]' ~/git/svc/sops/example.yaml`

Not sure if this is a simple documentation issue, or not "working as desired".

**For `--set`, the examples in README.rst are:**

`sops ~/git/svc/sops/example.yaml --set '["app2"]["key"]' '"app2keystringvalue"'`
`sops ~/git/svc/sops/example.yaml --set '["an_array"][1]' '"secretuser2"'`
and
`sops ~/git/svc/sops/example.yaml --set '["an_array"][1]' '{"uid1":null,"uid2":1000,"uid3":["bob"]}'`

For me, none of these work as described. Instead, the entire file is decrypted and opened in `vim` (my configured `$EDITOR`).  Here, unlike for `--extract`, I did not find any alternative order of params that works.

(If it helps, I'm on MacOS High Sierra, `gpg --version` "gpg (GnuPG) 2.2.4 libgcrypt 1.8.2", `sops -v` "sops 3.0.0")

issue_title: creation of datetime objects in riak ts record sets should be optional [JIRA: CLIENTS-832]
issue_body:
When returning record sets from Riak TS queries, the client automatically converts [Riak TS timestamps] (https://github.com/basho/riak-python-client/blob/ce5901fdde37efa2d3f576ac895b63457fe9a025/riak/transports/pbc/codec.py#L749) into `datetime` objects and vice versa. 

For many applications where Python is an intermediate layer, converting into a `datetime` is not required.

Additionally there are some timestamps that do not roundtrip properly.

The first of which is 1001 (code taken from https://github.com/basho/riak-python-client/blob/2.4.2/riak/transports/pbc/codec.py#L86 )

```
In [69]: d._unix_time_millis(d._datetime_from_unix_time_millis(1001))
Out[69]: 1000
```

It would be nice if the conversion to `datetime` objects as optional.

issue_title: cloudwatch logs filter_log_events paginator errors "same next token was received twice"
issue_body:
I believe this is related to https://github.com/aws/aws-cli/issues/3191 and that they are the same issue.
Running `boto3==1.7.30, botocore==1.10.30`

Suspected cause:
Some calls to CloudWatch Logs filter_log_events with a `nextToken` value return that same `nextToken` in the response.

In my particular case, these always seem to happen in situations in which there are no events in the payload -- the call checked a stream which had no matching data.


I had a monitoring script checking our log groups to see expected events every few minutes with filter_log_events.
Specifically,
```python
logclient = boto3.client('logs')
paginator = logclient.paginator('filter_log_events')
for page in paginator.paginate(
        logGroupName=group,
        startTime=monitor_start_window,
        filterPattern=monitor_filter):
    """ do fancy stuff """
```

when the volume of log events increased significantly, this started failing with
`Error during pagination: The same next token was received twice`

I found that this works fine:

```python
logclient = boto3.client('logs')
kwargs = {}
while True:
    response = logclient.filter_log_events(
        logGroupName=group,
        startTime=monitor_start_window,
        filterPattern=monitor_filter,
        **kwargs)
    """ do fancy stuff """
    if 'nextToken' not in response:
        break
    kwargs['nextToken'] = response['nextToken']
```

Some detailed logging reveals that the same token is sent twice in my case.
So, I have calls of the form `filter_log_events(..., nextToken='foobar123')` which return a response with `response['nextToken']='foobar123'`.

I don't know how boto3's paginators are implemented, but I suspect that's what's tripping things up.

Allowing the same token to be sent twice fixes it for me. I don't appear to receive duplicate logs, but that might be my good fortune.
For my use case, duplicates would not be problematic, so if anyone else is seeing this and looking for a solution... YMMV if you try this too.

In the pagination interface, there are a few extra parameters related to pagination that are snake cased (``max_items``, ``page_size``, ``starting_token``). These are different than the camel casing of the parameters that are actually sent in the request. So you would get something like this (using ``s3.list_objects``):
```
paginator.paginate(Bucket='mybucket', page_size=1)
```

Ideally we would want to change the pagiantion related parameters to camel case as well (``MaxItems``, ``PageSize``, ``StartingToken``) for the sake of consistency. So the above pagination call would instead be:
```
paginator.paginate(Bucket='mybucket', PageSize=1)
```

Let me know what you think.

issue_title: Galleria is always present and cannot be disabled in plonetruegallery
issue_body:
We prefer to use a different slideshow library in our installations and not allow our users to choose a different library we don't support. 

However collective.ptg.galleria is a hard dependency in de setup.py of collective.plonetruegallyer and the default value of display_type in IGallerySettings is galleria, thus Galleria is always there :-(

What would be the best route to support enabling this? We could make the dependency on galleria optional in the setup.py using an extras_require, but that would need an explicit warning on a new release that people should change the package include to collective.plonetruegallery[galleria].

Also the default value in IGallerySettings would have to be removed. The value list is already created using a vocabulary, so the default value would be the first item in the list of actually installed collective.ptg.* packages. 

My idea is to add an extra option in the generic plonetruegallery  control panel where you can select which of the found DisplayTypes should be activated and choosable. With an ordered in/out widget this could also solve the default item: move the default item to the top of the enabled list.  (A similar construct is used in for example collective.cove to select from the found available tiles in the application should be available to the users.)

issue_title: GitPython `repo.index.commit()` spawns persistent git.exe instance, holds handles to repo
issue_body:
I am trying to use GitPython for some repo manipulation, but ran into issues with my app, with handles open where i wouldn't expect.

Bug-jarring the issue, it seems that calling `repo.index.commit()` results in a several (4, consistently) git.exe processes being spawned, each holding a handle to the repo's root directory `newRepo`.  When the test below goes to delete this TempDir, the processes are still up, causing a failure on context-manager `__exit__()`.  My app occasionally needs to do a similar cleanup, so hits the same issue.

On the one hand, it looks like there is no contect-manager capable repo-wrapper, meaning it makes sense if _some_ resources is open, it will not typically be cleaned/GC'd before the tempdir `__exit__()`.  On the other hand - if `Repo` is going to behave like that, it really should not persist resources that have such side-effects.

Here is a working unittest:

<!-- language: lang-py -->

    import unittest
    import git
    import tempfile
    import os.path
    
    class Test(unittest.TestCase):
    
        def testCreateRepo(self):
            with tempfile.TemporaryDirectory(prefix=(__loader__.name) + "_") as mydir:
    
                # MAKE NEW REPO 
                repo = git.Repo.init(path=os.path.join(mydir, "newRepo"), mkdir=True)
                self.assertTrue(os.path.isdir(os.path.join(repo.working_dir, ".git")), "Failed to make new repo?")
                
                # MAKE FILE, COMMIT REPO
                testFileName = "testFile.txt"
                open(os.path.join(repo.working_dir, testFileName) , "w").close()
                repo.index.add([testFileName])
                self.assertTrue(repo.is_dirty())
                
                #### 
                # COMMENTING THIS OUT --> TEST PASSES
                repo.index.commit("added initial test file") 
                self.assertFalse(repo.is_dirty())
                #### 
                
                # adding this does not affect the handle
                git.cmd.Git.clear_cache()
                
                
                print("done") # exception thrown right after this, on __exit__
                # i can also os.walk(topdown=False) and delete all files and dirs (including .git/)
                # it is just the  newRepo/  folder itself who's handle is held open
                
    if __name__ == '__main__':
        unittest.main()

PermissionError: [WinError 32] The process cannot access the file because it is being used by another process: 'C:\\Users\\%USER%\\AppData\\Local\\Temp\\EXAMPLE_gitpython_v3kbrly_\\newRepo'

digging a little deeper, it seems that gitPython spawns multiple instances of git.exe processes, and each of them holds a handle to the _root_ folder of the repo `newRepo`.

- set a breakpoint immediately before the error, use sysinternals/handle to see open handles to `newRepo` ...  git.exe (4 separate PID's of git.exe to be precise)
- using sysinternals/procexp i can see that that they are all spawned from the python instance
-- I'm typically running this from PyDev, but i verified the issue reproduces under vanila command line invocation of python.exe as well
- the exception indicates it's a handle to `newRepo` being held.  Adding a little extra code to the above I think that is the only handle held.  I am able to successfully os.remove/os.rmdir() _every_ dir and file, including all of .git/; and i finally manually recreate the issue seen on __exit__() in my example when i `os.rmdir(newRepo)`

stepping through, it's the call to repo.index.commit() that actually leads to the the git.exe(s) being spawned.

issue_title: Meta: decide where we want to field usage questions
issue_body:
Chris Withers asked a [good question](https://mail.python.org/pipermail/distutils-sig/2018-January/031861.html) over on distutils-sig: where would we like folks to ask usage questions about pipenv?

At the moment we don't have anything in the README about that, so the answer defaults to either the issue tracker here, or else to one of the general PyPA mailing lists (i.e. distutils-sig or pypa-dev). None of those are especially great options longer term.

My own suggestion would be to create a `pipenv` tag on Stack Overflow and keep an eye on that ourselves (I have the required SO rep to create a tag for us if we decide to go this way), rather than creating yet another general chat channel to monitor. SO also has the advantage of more readily scaling future support efforts beyond the active `pipenv` contributors.

@pypa/pipenv-committers: Thoughts? Other suggestions?

issue_title: pxd files missing in the PyPI package
issue_body:
In the package currently available on PyPI (v1.1.4), the two .pxd files (c_numpy.pxd and c_python.pxd) in pyregion/ are missing.
It is thus not possible to generate _region_filter.c directly from _region_filter.pyx using Cython.
Instead, the .c file itself is shipped.

Please ensure that for the next releases, the pxd files are shipped, and not the c file.

It looks like that we have the tools to make old versions hidden on pypi, but not using it regularly. I think it may also affects other packages in the astropy ecosystem beyond ``astropy-helpers``, so we may need to revise the release instructions, too.

```
class AstropyRegister(SetuptoolsRegister):
    """Extends the built in 'register' command to support a ``--hidden`` option
    to make the registered version hidden on PyPI by default.

    The result of this is that when a version is registered as "hidden" it can
    still be downloaded from PyPI, but it does not show up in the list of
    actively supported versions under http://pypi.python.org/pypi/astropy, and
    is not set as the most recent version.

    Although this can always be set through the web interface it may be more
    convenient to be able to specify via the 'register' command.  Hidden may
    also be considered a safer default when running the 'register' command,
    though this command uses distutils' normal behavior if the ``--hidden``
    option is omitted.
    """
```

issue_title: threshold_niblack and threshold_sauvola sometimes return NaN when calling them with float64 images
issue_body:
## Description

`threshold_niblack` and `threshold_sauvola` sometimes return NaN when calling them with float64 images

## Way to reproduce
- [X] Code example

```
# the code below will return NaN values only.
# depending on the image's pixel values,
# it's possible to get back a mix of NaN and float values

import numpy as np
from skimage import filters
import skimage

# 16 pixels, single channel
src_img = np.array([0.03082192 + 2.19178082e-09] * 16).astype('float64')
src_img = src_img.reshape((4,4))

# will print array of 4x4 NaNs
print(filters.threshold_niblack(src_img))

# will print array of 4x4 NaNs
print(filters.threshold_sauvola(src_img))
```

the reason for the NaNs is that `sqrt` is being called in [_mean_std](https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/blob/master/skimage/filters/thresholding.py#L775) with negative numbers.

- [X] Operating system and version

OS X 10.11.6

- [X] Python version

2.7.10

- [X] scikit-image version (run `skimage.__version__`)

0.14dev

I am using the last commit as of now, da0800a2a0c4abb51c621ff495c3297cc69d43a1

### Problem

One would like to skip a specific upgrade and later maybe re-run it.

### Proposal

- [ ] `inveniomanage upgrader skip NAME` - add `NAME` upgrade to database
- [ ] `inveniomanage upgrader unskip NAME` - remove `NAME` upgrade to database

### Question

Do we need to distinguish between skipped and applied upgrades or we keep it on administrator? We could add a column `skipped` to `upgrade` table or allow `applied` to be `NULL`able.

There should be a usage guide which shows how to test invenio-github module locally using ngrok.

`psycopg2` requires Postgresql to be installed and thus `pip install -e .[all]` fails without it. The problem is that `run-tests.sh` requires datacite and sphinx packages, which are not installed with `pip install -e .[tests]`. As the tests can run with only sqlite we might want to either remove the dependencies from run-tests.py

After a discussion with @lnielsen we got to the point where:
- The module shouldn't depend on Postgresql to be installed on the laptop for running fast tests. Otherwise we can't test different versions of databases easily.
- The module needs to depend on Postgresql and Mysql for testing full compatibility.

Thus we could have two testing mode:
- Rapid: one for running tests rapidly with sqlite during development. It's dependencies can include optional features (example: `datacite`) as long as it does not make the tests too slow and does not require anything which is not installed with pypi. It would need `pip install -e .[tests]`.
- Full: used for travis and before merging a pull-request. It could be used in a docker container when run on dev computer. It would need `pip install -e .[all]` or something like `pip install -e .[full-tests]`.

issue_title: docs: add a Warning recommending not to store or advertise invenio-jsonschema URLs
issue_body:
Related to the discussion in inveniosoftware/invenio-records#117

**Problem Description**:
- records are versioned and can be reverted to a previous version.
- URLs can always change, be it the hostname or the API.

Thus we need to store references to JSON-Schemas as stable URLs. Example: DOI/EPIC/...

**Solution:**
We should add a warning in the documentation mentioning that we don't recommend storing invenio-jsonschema URLs directly in records. A more stable URI should be used.

issue_title: @base no longer prepended in local names
issue_body:
Hi,

I've noticed that the JSON-LD parser has changed its output w.r.t. prepending the @base URI to local names. The following code used to read [this file](https://gist.github.com/albertmeronyo/a2fff3db14362d539a1d99598476c92b#file-test-csv-metadata-json)

```
from rdflib import Graph

g = Graph()
g.parse('test.csv-metadata.json', format='json-ld')
```

and the resulting graph contained the triple 
```
N86eb3d54036d4bb98a416ec6022be35b http://www.w3.org/ns/csvw#aboutUrl https://iisg.amsterdam/resource/{_row}
```

However this has changed and now the triple is
```
N86eb3d54036d4bb98a416ec6022be35b http://www.w3.org/ns/csvw#aboutUrl {_row}
```

So it seems either this specific property is not expected to be a URI anymore (but a literal instead), or the parser is not prepending @base to URIs.

Could you help me out with this?

Thanks,
Albert

Allow reconfiguration of Patroni using the api
issue_body:
Allow users to call the api to change configuration.

For this, we may need to change the way we currently start postgres, as we do it like this:

    postgres --listen_addresses=[] --port=[] [--setting=value]

As these parameters will always take precedence above configuration file values, this doesn't play nicely with being able to change the configuration with the api.

Proposal:

Only provide really necessary options to postgres, I think the following 2 are essential, as Patroni needs to be able to connect to and advertise the PostgreSQL location:

* `listen_addresses`
* `port`

Non-essential options for Patroni would be written in `patroni.conf` which is included in `postgresql.conf`.

We may wish to write global configuration to the DCS, as replica's created from backups may not have all configuration changes.

Individual instances may still be configured differently, this would be done using `ALTER SYSTEM`.

The configuration precedence would then be something like this:

1. `postgresql.conf`
2. Configuration from DCS (written to `patroni.conf`)
3. Configuration from `postgresql.auto.conf`
4. Command Line parameters essential to Patroni

issue_body: I have a bash prompt defined in **~/.bashrc** as such: PS1=$(printf '%*s\n' "${COLUMNS:-$(tput cols)}" '' | tr ' ' _)'\n\[\e[32m\]\u@\h\[\e[0m\] \[\e[33m\]\w\[\e[0m\] <\[\e[36m\]$(git symbolic-ref HEAD 2>/dev/null | cut -d"/" -f 3)$(svn info 2>/dev/null | sed -n "/URL:/s/.*\///p" | head -n 1 )\[\e[0m\]>\n> \[\e[33m\]\t\[\e[0m\] \[\e[36m\]\j \[\e[33m\]\s\[\e[0m\] \$ ' The result of "**tput cols**" at evaluation time of **~/.bashrc** (setting up the prompt to P1) when hitting **F12** seems to be **80**, afterwards "**tput cols**" returns the correct value (in my case 277). When launching a new bash instance within that existing one, "tput cols" within evaluation of **~/.bashrc** correctly returns 277. Maybe we have a bug upon first initializing a bash within guake at evaluation time of **~/.bashrc** (resulting in an ugly prompt in my case). issue_title: Set check_update to False by default
issue_body:
Currently the value is set to True by default which means it will issue an HTTPS request to pypi each time the client is initiated plus it polutes the output with the following misleading comment:

    You are running an outdated version of JIRA Python %s. Current version is %s. Do not file any bugs against older versions.

Misleading because the version is not outdated, it is the version I've tested my software against and the users MUST NOT upgrading it as long as they want support for my software. Issuing an HTTPS request on client instantiation does not makes sense IMHO - from the technical perspective as from the privacy perspective. I don't want that someone can track when I call a program which is using the library and I also don't want a program that performs an HTTPS request before it is starting to do it's millisecond work plus emiting a warning that it cannot reach the internet where it is meant to run as an intranet application.

To specify requirements with a specific version using requirements.txt and then running it in a virtualenv is one of the common ways to deploy python applications. IMHO the version check is contra productive because of this and I've never seen a library which is doing such a thing.

Please set it to `False` by default or remove it completely.

issue_title: state.generate_testcase does not use the filename provided
issue_body:
### Manticore version
Version: 0.1.7 (48f88be7590c7b15ff27cfd8c2da883d71e98190)

### Summary of the problem
`state.generate_testcase` does not use the filename provided to generate the files.
This was tested only for smart contracts.

### Step to reproduce the behavior
```
# simple.py
from manticore.ethereum import ManticoreEVM

m = ManticoreEVM() # initiate the blockchain
source_code ='''
pragma solidity^0.4.18;
contract Simple {
    function f(uint a) payable {
        if (a == 0x41) {
            throw;
        }
    }
}
'''

# Initiate the accounts
user_account = m.create_account(balance=1000)
contract_account = m.solidity_create_contract(source_code, owner=user_account, balance=0)

# Call f(a), with a symbolic value
contract_account.f(m.SValue)

# Check if an execution ends with a REVERT or INVALID
for state in m.terminated_states: 
    last_tx = state.platform.transactions[-1]
    if last_tx.result in ['REVERT','INVALID']:
        print "Error found in f() execution (see %s)"%m.workspace

        # filename should be named SimpleBug_00000000.tx
        state.generate_testcase('SimpleBug')
        break
```
`python simple.py` should generate files named "SimpleBug_*", but "test_*" is used insted

### Expected behavior
Generation of mcore_*/SimpleBug* files

### Actual behavior
Generation of mcore_*/test* files

### Any relevant logs
If I use ``m._generate_testcase_callback(state, 'SimpleBug','message')`` and this fix: https://github.com/montyly/manticore/commit/4f3a7ee059f6225bbe51275594e3c14a836a0795

issue_title: Let NEXRADLevel2File's filename be a .gz file
issue_body:
All of the level 2 archive files that I've seen from [NOAA's data archive site](http://www.ncdc.noaa.gov/nexradinv/) have been .gz files.  Would it be worth detecting the filename's extension, and if it is a .gz file opening it with `gzip.open` instead of just using open?  As it is, you can work around this by using an open .gz file as the filename.  I can submit a pull request if I need to; it will basically only add a couple lines in `NEXRADLevel2File.__init__`:

    if filename.endswith('.gz'):  # maybe should use os.path.splitext()[-1] == '.gz' to be cross-platform?
        fh = gzip.open(filename, 'rb') # 'rb' is the default, don't really need it...

and it would need to add the relevant imports, too.

As an aside, everywhere I've seen on NOAA's website mentions that the radar data is stored as a bzipped file, but all of the ones I've seen have been gzipped.  Granted, I've only worked with a single data set from KTLX.  Are the others different?

issue_title: SNS Subscription appears to be incomplete
issue_body:
I'm working on a project using AWS::SNS::Subscription

Here's the CFN documentation for reference:
http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSCloudFormation/latest/UserGuide/aws-resource-sns-subscription.html#cfn-sns-topicarn

Reviewing the troposphere/sns.py it looks like Subscription is incomplete:
```
class Subscription(AWSProperty):
    props = {
        'Endpoint': (basestring, True),
        'Protocol': (basestring, True),
    }
```
I believe this should be:
```
class Subscription(AWSObject):
    resource_type = "AWS::SNS::Subscription"

    props = {
        'Endpoint': (basestring, True),
        'Protocol': (basestring, True),
        'TopicArn': (basestring, True),
    }
```

I've tested this in my CFN templates and it's worked as expected.  I can do a pull request if you prefer.  This seemed small enough that maybe someone could just sneak it in :)

issue_title: Python chroot cache invalidation broken when sources of dependencies are changed.
issue_body:
I'll link to a repo that can reproduce this issue in a few minutes.  Essentially what we have observed is that repeatedly running:

`./pants run <python binary target` will re-use the same cached chroot regardless of if dependencies of that binary target sources change.  This obviously can lead to issues.

Sometimes this issue is masked by the fact that the chroots themselves *may* be implemented via hardlinks, but not always.

It's now effectively empty.

Should also take the opportunity to change the API for `Scheduler.schedule(execution)` to `Scheduler.execute(execution)`, and have it directly return the resulting root values.

This would also be a good opportunity to merge: `build_request`, `execution_request`, `selection_request`, `schedule` into just `execute`.

Additionally, having `BuildResult.errors` and also `Throw` values inside the result is annoying. The former could probably become a synchronous exception.

issue_title: Support segregation of `resolve.npm` results by arch
issue_body:
As @huckphin pointed out, right now Medium has a huge time sink problem with plain-old-npm since npm does not appear to bake arch info into native packages.  This means that builds on dev machines - say OS X - cannot share resolve results with CI machines or production - say Linux.  Pants should provide the option or always segregate resolve results by architechture.  IOW the arch should be part of the NpmResolve task's cache key.  At worst this is slower for pure js builds where segregation was not needed, but with the feature, expensive resolves can be shared via the distributed cache.

Not sure if @chrispesto has run into this, but I'm guessing this is a feature Twitter would also leverage to allow safe remote artifact cache sharing.

issue_title: Swagger DefinitionBody Error: map keys must be strings; received numeric [200] instead
issue_body:
<!--

Before reporting a new issue, make sure we don't have any duplicates already open or closed by
searching the issues list. If there is a duplicate, re-open or add a comment to the
existing issue instead of creating a new one. If you are reporting a bug,
make sure to include relevant information asked below to help with debugging.

## GENERAL HELP QUESTIONS ##
Github Issues is for bug reports and feature requests. If you have general support
questions, the following locations are a good place:

- Post a question in StackOverflow  with "aws-sam" tag

-->

**Description:**

<!-- Briefly describe the problem you are facing -->
Transformation to cloudformation template converts '200' to 200 causing an invalid template error.

Inside my serverless.template.yaml file the following is transformed into an invalid cloudformation segment.

responses:
  '200':
    description: "200 response"
    schema:
      $ref: "#/definitions/Empty"
    headers:
      Access-Control-Allow-Origin:
        type: "string"
      Access-Control-Allow-Methods:
        type: "string"
      Access-Control-Allow-Headers:
        type: "string"

becomes 

responses:
  200:  
    description: 200 response
    schema:
      $ref: '#/definitions/Empty'
    headers:
      Access-Control-Allow-Origin:
        type: string
      Access-Control-Allow-Methods:
        type: string
      Access-Control-Allow-Headers:
        type: string
**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. add the following Resource to SAM yaml template and try to deploy it using the sam cli:

RestApi:
  Type: AWS::Serverless::Api
  Properties:
    StageName: prod
    DefinitionBody:
      swagger: "2.0"
      info:
        version: "2018-04-18T18:37:10Z"
        title: "MyTitle"
      host: "${myurl}.execute-api.${region}.amazonaws.com"
      basePath: "/prod"
      schemes:
      - "https"
      paths:
        /:
          post:
            produces:
            - "application/json"
            responses:
              '200':
                description: "200 response"
                schema:
                  $ref: "#/definitions/Empty"
          options:
            consumes:
            - "application/json"
            produces:
            - "application/json"
            responses:
              '200':
                description: "200 response"
                schema:
                  $ref: "#/definitions/Empty"
                headers:
                  Access-Control-Allow-Origin:
                    type: "string"
                  Access-Control-Allow-Methods:
                    type: "string"
                  Access-Control-Allow-Headers:
                    type: "string"
      definitions:
        Empty:
          type: "object"
          title: "Empty Schema"

**Observed result:**

the generated cloudformation template creates a dictionary key of 200 for the responses object which causes the following error:

map keys must be strings; received numeric [200] instead

**Expected result:**
The dictionary key should stay the same in the generated template as it is in the source template.

issue_title: Allow arbitrary named hook functions for Pre and Post Hook feature of Safe Deployments.
issue_body:
If you need to use the Pre or Post Hook feature of the [Safe Deployments](https://github.com/awslabs/serverless-application-model/blob/master/docs/safe_lambda_deployments.rst#pretraffic-posttraffic-hooks), you need to name the hook function with a prefix (CodeDeployHook_). This is not super clear and breaks the mental model customers have on naming CloudFormation resources. 

Option 1:
To make this feature more flexible and allow customers to follow the naming schema's they wish, I am proposing to add a [Policy Template](https://github.com/awslabs/serverless-application-model/blob/master/docs/policy_templates.rst). This would be mostly the same IAM Role CodeDeploy needs that takes the hook functions in as a parameter.

Option 2:
SAM could create the CodeDeploy IAM Role and inject the Lambda Function arns into the policy. In the `DeploymentPreference` block, customers are already defining the functions for the pre and post hook.

Thoughts?

issue_title: Separate test code and test data
issue_body:
I have noticed that the test code is messy because it includes the hard-coded data. Not only is this bad practice, it also makes it harder to add test cases (e.g. for non-compliant events, see #545).

Pytest has a `@pytest.mark.parametrize` decorator to parameterize test functions, but the data still has to be embedded in the code. A better solution would be having an external tests.yml file, from which the data is loaded. It could look like [this](https://gist.github.com/ghcs27/16740403c309403c7ede91ca0e4ee8f5#file-tests-yml).

[Here](https://gist.github.com/ghcs27/2ddc983cdcebb695d4b22dbd9a41de8d#file-load_test_data-py)'s a gist for parameterizing the test from a YAML file.

issue_title: DPH joins the team of maintainers
issue_body:
Hello everyone

Please help me in welcoming DPH to the maintainers team of SoCo/SoCo.

@DPH has been involved with the SoCo projects for a long while now and has contributed in many different areas. Especially, he has been very active with issues; reproducing, testing patches, asking for clarification and doing research on possible solutions. All these contributions have consistently been of high quality and has helped push forward.

Furthermore, @DPH is friendly, objective and on-point with the communication, which makes him a good fit for open source development.

issue_title: Make sign in page more responsive.
issue_body:
## Summary

* Sign in fields are hidden due to a bird and a lot of white space. See gif below.
* Some overlap.

## System information

 - Version: Develop
 - Operating system: iOS
 - Browser: Safari

![ios](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7193975/31412350-94db14a2-adc9-11e7-89f4-5a4e8b4ecd5f.gif)

Current syntax looks like this:

```
  {% frontend_assets 'kolibri_core' plugin='kolibri.core' %}
```

It would be nice if we made it match python import syntax, so it looks like this:

```
  {% frontend_assets 'kolibri.core.KolibriCoreFrontend' %}
```

That should make the `name` attribute for `KolibriFrontendPluginBase` unnecessary.

### Observed behavior

note extra spacing

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2367265/42341469-054d7328-8048-11e8-85cd-d7d134abbdcc.png)

### Expected behavior

standard layout

### User-facing consequences

inconsistent/ugly layout

### Steps to reproduce

go to import content

### Context

0.11.0

issue_title: Should we do separate repositories for the different APIs?
issue_body:
@e-lo asked:

Where does API / Examples / Gists live?

Preferably in separate repositories, tagged with OMX version #.  Currently the APIs of all code are in one place and move forward in commits together. I personally find this messy.   

What do you all think about having:
• OMX.r
• OMX.py
• OMX.cpp
• OMX.java
• etc...
all as separate repositories?  There are [good and bad things](http://programmers.stackexchange.com/questions/161293/choosing-between-single-or-multiple-projects-in-a-git-repository) about this, but the main issue i see is one of the APIs moving forward w/out volunteers to move forward the other APIs and ended up with stale parts of a repo.  The major disadvantage I see is consolidated issue tracking/version control.

ITSM that ExaBGP requires the IP address of a neighbor to be defined in the configuration. For certain scenarios it can be useful to define any IP or that the peer must be within a certain prefix but that we don't have to define each individual neighbor. Some vendors already support this;

issue_title: exec_start with detach=True and stream=True not working as expected
issue_body:
Maybe I'm doing something wrong or do not understand the underlying API but here it goes.
I want to launch a command on a container in the background and read the stream of the command result. A microtest would be:
```
from docker import Client

cli = Client(base_url='unix://var/run/docker.sock')

exec_id = cli.exec_create(
        'container_name', 
        'timeout 10 sh -c "sleep 5 && echo loreipsudeim"', 
        stdout=True,
        stderr=True)
generator = cli.exec_start(exec_id, detach=True, stream=True)

for x in generator:
    print x
```

From the docker-py documentation, it seems that when stream=True, a blocking generator is returned. I'd expect the generator to block until there's no output from the command but that's not the case. What should be the expected behavior?

Is it supported the use case I propose?

I'm on docker 1.6.2 (seen this in 1.7 too) and docker-py 1.2.3.

The image is 1.516GB so I'm assuming we are running out of memory (this process is within a container).

Can the save be done directly to disk and avoid being pushed into memory?

issue_title: Distinguish “matched, value is None” versus “failed to match”
issue_body:
The jmespath.search function returns None in two distinct cases:

```
>>> import jmespath

>>> foo = {'bar': {'lorem': 13, 'ipsum': None}}
>>> repr(jmespath.search('bar.lorem', foo))
'13'
>>> repr(jmespath.search('bar.ipsum', foo))    # Path matches, value None
'None'
>>> repr(jmespath.search('dolor', foo))    # Path does not match
'None'
```
It appears the JMESPath search API returns None in these two distinct cases. How can the caller know the difference between them?

I would expect no return in the case of a match failure, and instead an exception (such as KeyError or ValueError).

issue_title: Do not automatically scale up to 1 after a deploy
issue_body:
If a user has 0 containers of an app running and publishes a new release, we should confirm that Deis doesn't automatically scale up to 1. 

**Example:**
I have an app that defines two proctypes, **web** and **foo**. I keep my **web** scaled to 0, and only want **foo** to be running most of the time. If I deploy my app again, will Deis scale **web** back to 1?

[This](https://github.com/deis/deis/commit/19b06d5ae3652e4572c0b8803a4377d7221cbe62) commit seems to have left aws provisioning via the contrib scripts in an inconsistent state.

In master, the [health check](http://docs.deis.io/en/latest/installing_deis/quick-start/#get-the-source) has been changed to reference 9090, but the user-data installs deisctl 1.10.1, the units of which doesnt expose 9090 yet.

The result is that using the /opt/bin/deisctl binary to install the platform will result in a cluster that cant be used, since the ELB will permanently kick out all the nodes.

This could be confusing for first-time installers.

issue_title: store: Consider switching to official ceph-docker images
issue_body:
The Ceph team (with the great @ulexus, whose work our deis-store is based on) recently started publishing official Docker images for Ceph: https://github.com/ceph/ceph-docker

We should consider switching to them, as presumably they know more about Ceph than we do :)

Most interesting to me is the [config](https://github.com/ceph/ceph-docker/tree/master/config) container, which sets up the cluster and uses etcd(!) to manage cluster configuration.

example.com/blog/ -> blog.service
example.com/ -> example.com.service

issue_title: Upgrade registry to docker-registry v2 (Docker Distribution)
issue_body:
There are several issues relating to registry performance (#3580, #3479).

We should upgrade to the Go-based rewrite of the [official Docker registry](https://docs.docker.com/registry/), which is now called [Docker distribution](https://github.com/docker/distribution). We'll also need to patch in our changes to support the `deis pull` workflow.

Additionally, there is also a [native driver](https://github.com/docker/distribution/blob/master/registry/storage/driver/rados/rados.go) to consume Ceph RADOS which is likely going to be more performant than our current S3-on-Ceph implementation. We should investigate this, although it is likely to complicate our upgrade path.

Upgrading to registry v2 also allows us to support Docker 1.6-built images, resolving issues like #3550.

issue_title: Fast sync may leave DB in inconsistent state
issue_body:
The fast-sync is composed of two steps: 
1. Fetch/store chain data (headers, bodies and receipts), in batches, until we are in sync with the network
2. Fetch/store state data by requesting all missing trie nodes starting from the current head's state_root

Right now, step 1 advances our chain head for every batch it downloads/stores, so if we don't complete step 2 we're left with a DB in an inconsistent state as we won't have the state for the current head.

One easy way to workaround that is to check (in `FullNodeSyncer`) if we have the state for the current head before running a regular sync, and, if not run the `StateDownloader` before starting the regular sync.

issue_title: unnecessary test for write permission on `pkgs` and `conda-meta` in `conda create`
issue_body:
**I'm submitting a...** - [X] bug report - [] feature request ## Current Behavior When creating a new conda environment with `conda create -n `, if there is not write permission under the `pkgs` or `conda-meta` subdirectories of the root environment, then it considers that the installation does not have sufficient write permission, so offers to create the environment under `$HOME/.conda/envs` instead of under `/envs` -- even though neither of those subdirectories actually needs to be writable. It may be desirable to provide a conda installation where only the `envs` directory is writable (in order to prevent accidental installation into the root environment). However, currently in such a setup, `conda create -n ` cannot be used to create a new environment under `/envs/` (although `-p ` can be used). ### Steps to Reproduce ``` chmod -R a-w chmod u+w /envs conda create -n my-conda-env ``` This gives: ``` ## Package Plan ## environment location: /.conda/envs/my-conda-env Proceed ([y]/n)? ``` ## Expected Behavior ``` ## Package Plan ## environment location: /envs/my-conda-env Proceed ([y]/n)? ``` ## Environment Information (note the following local system info is redacted: leading path elements, uid, gid)

issue_title: Conda 4.2.x may hang after install / update operations on Windows
issue_body: Conda may try to clean up some trash as a background operation when running other commands, but it probably shouldn't sleep or wait doing so, in the event the cleaning fails.

issue_title: Incorrect read coordinates and incorrect TLEN after Fetching alignment record from a BAM file
issue_body:
Hi Pysam team,
I just installed  version pysam==0.9.1.4, and see the following problem while capturing the read lines. The CHROM and POS for both the read and its mate are shifted by one (1) from the original line in the BAM file.
So the **columns 3, 4, 7 and 8 are incorrect**. 

Also, the TLEN or column 9 is totally different from the original value. In the example below you can see that the original value is -872 and was converted to 57.

Furthermore, using the code x.next or x.rnext, the returned values are correct, that is identical to the ones in the BAM, but  using the code x.start or x.next_start give the incorrect coordinates. (see code below for details).  So chromosomes names are ok but not their coordinates.

BAM files are supposed to be 0-based; In the pysam documentation, the display of coordinates is said to be a 0-based (inclusive) information, so I guess as I have used a BAM file as input, I should not have any discrepancies between the coordinates from the BAM and the one showed by pysam. 

I used python 3.5.0 and pysam 0.9.1.4. and used the following code to extract the alignment records:

```
import pysam
bamf = pysam.AlignmentFile(mybam, "rb")
iter = bamf.fetch(chr, pos, pos+100)
for x in iter: 
    print(str(x))
    print(type(x))
    print("x.rnext \t" + str(bamf.get_reference_name(x.rnext)))
    print("x.rname \t" + str(bamf.getrname(x.rname)))
    print("x.start \t" + str(x.reference_start))
    print("x.next_start \t" + str(x.next_reference_start))
```
## Examples

**Here the line extracted and printed by pysam:**
D87P8DQ1:274:C59G0ACXX:5:2315:19806:57716       83      **20**      33157236        0       29S57M  **20**      33156421        **57**      CTCCCTCGACTCCAGCCGCATCCCCTCCCCCACCACCACCACCACCACCACCACCACCACCACCACCACCACCACCACCACCATCA       array('B', [2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 30, 29, 29, 32, 30, 25, 30, 31, 28, 16, 32, 29, 31, 29, 28, 6, 31, 27, 25, 31, 27, 31, 31, 29, 25, 31, 28, 30, 31, 29, 31, 31, 30, 31, 31, 30, 31, 31, 30, 31, 31, 31, 31, 32, 28, 31, 32, 31, 32, 31, 31, 27, 31, 29])  [('MC', '1S57M28S'), ('BD', 'IIIIONMMLLKKJJJJJJIIIIIIIIIIIIIIMIKLHKLHKLHKLHKLHKLHKLHKLHKLHKLHKLHKLILMILNJMOLPQRLMII'), ('MD', '54C2'), ('PG', 'MarkDuplicates'), ('RG', 'C59G0ACXX_5'), ('BI', 'LLLLSRQPPOOONNNMMMMMMLLLLLLLLLLLNLPNLOMKOMKOMKOMKOMKOMKOMKOMKOMKOMKOMKOMKOMLPNMQPRPPLL'), ('NM', 1), ('MQ', 0), ('AS', 54), ('XS', 57)]

x.rnext  21
x.rname  21
x.start  33157236
x.next_start  33156421

**Here the original line in the Bam file:**
D88P9DQ1:274:C59G0ACXX:5:2315:19806:57716       83      **21**      33157237        **0**       29S57M  =       33156422        **-872**    CTCCCTCGACTCCAGCCGCATCCCCTCCCCCACCACCACCACCACCACCACCACCACCACCACCACCACCACCACCACCACCATCA       ################################?>>A?:?@=1A>@>='@<:@<@@>:@=?@>@@?@@?@@?@@@@ A= @ A@A@@@  MC:Z:1S57M28S   BD:Z:IIIIONMMLLKKJJJJJJIIIIIIIIIIIIIIMIKLHKLHKLHKLHKLHKLHKLHKLHKLHKLHKLHKLILMILNJMOLPQRLMII  MD:Z:54C2       PG:Z:MarkDuplicates     RG:Z:C59G0ACXX_5        BI:Z:LLLLSRQPPOOONNNMMMMMMLLLLLLLLLLLNLPNLOMKOMKOMKOMKOMKOMKOMKOMKOMKOMKOMKOMKOMLPNMQPRPPLL     NM:i:1  MQ:i:0  AS:i:54 XS:i:57

Let us know if you have any questions or comments.
Thanks,
Best,
Chris.

issue_title: Content-MD5 header does not validate properly for blob uploads in chunks
issue_body:
### Description:
I would like to use MD5 validation to ensure my data survives transport when I upload blobs to Azure storage. Per the documentation, I supply a content-MD5 header along with my upload, so that the service validates the incoming file against this header.

This works great for small files. **But once I try to upload files that require chunked uploads -- e.g. I'm currently trying to upload a 1GB file -- the validation is always unsuccessful.** I'm using `BlobService.put_block_blob_from_path(...)` for the upload.

For reference, here is how I calculate the MD5 locally and pass it along with my upload.

```
# md5 generation for validating uploads
import hashlib
def md5_for_file(f, block_size=2**20):
    md5 = hashlib.md5()
    while True:
        data = f.read(block_size)
        if not data:
            break
        md5.update(data)
    return md5.digest().encode('base64')[:-1] 
    
# do the uploads, with file path p
for p in paths:
    blob_name = path_leaf(p)
    original_md5 = md5_for_file(open(p, 'rb'))
    blob_service.put_block_blob_from_path(storage_container_name, blob_name, p, content_md5=original_md5) # specify md5 to do an md5 check automatically
```

I get a WindowsAzureError when I run the above, due to the MD5 not validating properly:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "azureUpload.py", line 44, in <module>
    blob_service.put_block_blob_from_path(storage_container_name, blob_name, p, content_md5=original_md5) # specify md5 to do an md5 check automatically
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/azure/storage/blobservice.py", line 764, in put_block_blob_from_path
    progress_callback)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/azure/storage/blobservice.py", line 861, in put_block_blob_from_file
    x_ms_lease_id)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/azure/storage/blobservice.py", line 696, in put_blob
    self._perform_request(request)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/azure/storage/storageclient.py", line 150, in _perform_request
    _storage_error_handler(ex)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/azure/storage/__init__.py", line 889, in _storage_error_handler
    return _general_error_handler(http_error)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/azure/__init__.py", line 931, in _general_error_handler
    http_error.respbody.decode('utf-8'))
azure.WindowsAzureError
```

# The issue

My local MD5 computation isn't the issue -- I've checked it against Azure with small uploads, and it produces the same result as md5sum for my 1GB file.

**How should big files be uploaded with MD5 validation?** It seems that the API should be changed so that MD5 is computed for each chunk.

# How to reproduce

Create a >64MB file using `dd if=/dev/urandom of=65M.random bs=1M count=65`, then upload it with the code above. You get the same traceback as I did.

But if you chop 20MB off that using `dd if=/dev/urandom of=45M.random bs=1M count=45`, we won't trigger chunking, and the upload succeeds, along with MD5 validation.

If you remove MD5 validation (don't specify `content_md5` parameter), both uploads succeed.

Thus, the issue lies with MD5 validation with files that trigger chunking.

##### Original Issue: https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/issues/264
### Comments (2):
#### @huguesv commented on 2015-01-05 15:57:16:
> Thanks for letting us know about this issue.

The documentation for Put Block List REST API says:
"Note that this header is associated with the request content, and not with the content of the blob itself."

So passing in the MD5 of the whole blob to Put Block List is definitely the wrong thing to do.

We should calculate the MD5 for each chunk and pass it to Put Block (Put Blob in the case of no chunking), if the user wants validation.  The content\_md5 parameter should be changed to a Boolean, matching the behavior of the useTransactionalMD5 parameter used in Node.js SDK:
https://github.com/Azure/azure-storage-node/blob/master/lib/services/blob/blobservice.js

All Page Blob and Block Blob APIs should be reviewed for this, both upload and download.


#### @maximz commented on 2015-01-06 18:44:13:
> @huguesv Thanks for the update. Let me know if there's any further way I can help. (For now, I've just disabled MD5 verification in my code)
Look for recent runs with identical blob hashes, and re-use their results within some single-digit number of days.

issue_title: detect_aws_credentials hook raises a false positive match when AWS credential values contain only a space character(s)

The detect_aws_credentials hook raises a false positive match when AWS credential values contain only a space character(s). For example, if the ~/.aws/credentials file contains the following:

[test]
aws_access_key_id = ggfgafg
aws_secret_access_key =

```
Detect AWS Credentials...................................................Failed
hookid: detect-aws-credentials

AWS secret found in test.txt: ****************************
```

Which is technically true, but in essence the hook should trim (remove) the space(s) in the values for the variables prior to checking the file(s) to prevent this confusing issue from occurring.

issue_body:
I'd like to address two issues/questions:

1. Suppose I feed multiple observation sequences, is the returned log likelihood value of ```score()```/```decode()``` the sum of log likelihood of the observation sequences or the average of them? I don't see a clear statement on the document at http://hmmlearn.github.io/hmmlearn/generated/hmmlearn.hmm.GaussianHMM.html.

2. Log likelihood is supposed to be a negative value in any ways. Right? Otherwise, I'll convert to a probability value always greater than 1, which doesn't make sense. Why I always got a positive log likelihood value after calling ```fit()```?

I am using the client to upload a file using `FormData`, almost exactly the same code as shown in [this example](http://aiohttp.readthedocs.io/en/stable/client.html#post-a-multipart-encoded-file). The main difference is that I am passing a file created by [tempfile.TemporaryFile](https://docs.python.org/3/library/tempfile.html) as the second argument to `add_field`.

The result is: `TypeError: Can not serialize value type: <class '_io.BufferedRandom'>
 headers: {'Content-Type': 'application/vnd.ms-excel'}
 value: <_io.BufferedRandom name=23>`

The root exception is actually thrown from [`helpers.guess_filename`](https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/blob/master/aiohttp/helpers.py#L280): `TypeError: 'int' object is not subscriptable`

## Expected behaviour

There should be no need to call `guess_filename` because I specified a filename:
`form.add_field('file', data, filename=filename, content_type=content_type)`

And even if I didn't provide a filename, `guess_filename` should not throw an exception if `name` is not a string (which is apparently the case for tempfiles; notice that my tempfile has the number 23 for a name).

## Actual behaviour

[`FormData._gen_form_data`](https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/blob/master/aiohttp/formdata.py#L115-L117) is not passing the filename to `payload.get_payload`, even though the filename is available. It seems like the code could be changed to include `filename=dispparams.get('filename')` in the kwargs for `get_payload`.

`guess_filename` assumes that `name` will be a string. Oddly enough, sometimes it's a number.

## Steps to reproduce

Here is my code that produces the error. `data` is a `tempfile.TemporaryFile()`. The exception is thrown from the `post` call. `url`, `filename`, and `content_type` are all valid strings.

```python
    async def post_temp_file(self, url, filename, content_type, data):
        form = FormData()
        form.add_field('file', data, filename=filename, content_type=content_type)

        async with self.rest.post(url, data=form) as response:
            await self._raise_for_status(response)
            body = await response.text()

        return json.loads(body)
```

### Workaround

```python
        f = BytesIO(data.read())
        setattr(f, 'filename', filename)
        form.add_field('file', f, filename=filename, content_type=content_type)
```

## Your environment

Python 3.6.1 on Ubuntu

## Long story short

Random aiohttp crashes

## Expected behaviour

That there wouldn't be an error.

## Actual behaviour

It crashes after a random amount of time.

## Steps to reproduce

aiohttp server:
```
from aiohttp import web
import socketio

sio = socketio.AsyncServer()
app = web.Application()
sio.attach(app)

connections = { }

# On client connect
@sio.on('connect')
def connect(sid, environ):
    print("New connection ", "(" + sid + ")")

# On client disconnect
@sio.on('disconnect')
def disconnect(sid):
    print('Disconnected ', "(" + sid + ")")

# Say Hi to eachother
@sio.on('hello')
def hello(sid, data):
    connections[sid] = data
    print("hello ", "I am " + data + " with sid: " + sid)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    web.run_app(app)
```

Working with visual studio 2017
Installed:
- Python
- Aiohttp
- python-socketio

issue_title: Add support for Django 1.10's new style middleware
issue_body:
Once #18 is merged, Travis will test django-cors-middleware under Django 1.10a1, with the middleware specified via the new `MIDDLEWARE` setting rather than the deprecated `MIDDLEWARE_CLASSES`. 

This currently fails, so a later PR will need to:
1) Use `django.utils.deprecation.MiddlewareMixin`
2) Decide how to handle non-HTTP200 responses, since the behaviour has now changed (ie unless special handling is added to django-cors-middleware, it will stop adding CORS headers to the responses of non-preflight requests - see the comment for the `test_middleware_integration_get_auth_view` test added in #18 for details). This may require subclassing `ExceptionMiddleware`.

For more info, see:
https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.10/topics/http/middleware/#upgrading-pre-django-1-10-style-middleware

Idea: add a GenotypeAlleleCountsArray class to the model API, to support arrays where genotype calls are represented as the allele counts per call. E.g., a diploid hom ref 0/0 call becomes (2, 0), a diploid het 0/1 call becomes (1, 1), a triploid het call 0/0/1 becomes (2, 1), etc. Array shape is regular regardless of ploidy, i.e., (n_variants, n_samples, n_alleles). Ploidy is obtained by summing over the last (n_alleles) dimension.

The rationale is to provide a direct pathway for analysing genotype data from individuals where ploidy may vary from one chromosome to another, or within the same chromosome, or where ploidy may vary from one individual to another. The new class could provide some convenience methods analogous to GenotypeArray, e.g., count_alleles() and count_alleles_subpops().

See also alimanfoo/vcfnp#63.

issue_title: When used with get_mock_object, DateTimeType returns naive datetime
issue_body:
Fairly straightforward to reproduce:

```
In [1]: from schematics.types import DateTimeType

In [2]: from schematics.models import Model

In [11]: class Toaster(Model):
    created_at = DateTimeType(required=True)

In [15]: Toaster.get_mock_object().created_at.tzinfo

In [16]:
```

Since people might have assumed this would always be the case, perhaps an extra constructor arguments `default_timezone` would be useful? This would make sure that only timezone-aware datetime are returned. It would use the timezone on the parsed datetime if it's there, or the default one.

Thoughts?
