issue_title: Check additional environment variables for credentials
issue_body:
When using Jenkins declarative pipelines, credentials can be set as environment variables as follows:

```groovy
environment { 
    SAUCELABS = credentials('SAUCELABS') 
}
```

For credentials which are of type "Standard username and password", the environment variable specified will be set to `username:password` and two additional environment variables will be automatically be defined: `MYVARNAME_USR` and `MYVARNAME_PSW` respectively.

It would be convenient for pytest-selenium to therefore check `SAUCELABS_USR` and `SAUCELABS_PSW` for Sauce Labs, and similar for `BROWSERSTACK`, `TESTINGBOT`, and `CROSSBROWSERTESTING`.

See https://jenkins.io/doc/pipeline/tour/environment/ for more details.

issue_title: Creating documentation requires application already installed
issue_body:
The makefile to create the man pages requires the application to already be installed.

Unfortunately this creates a fairly circular dependency that is an issue in packaging for Fedora/EPEL 

Due to the way rpmbuild works there is no time letsencrypt, jws or letsencrypt-renew will be on the path to be called to capture the --help output unless it is already actually installed in the mock environment.

Is there a specific reason sphinxcontrib.programoutput was selected to created the man page or is it feasible to generate a man page without the application from being installed in the first place?

Calling make in the %install  section with PATH manipulation to build the man pages is not very clean and it would be far nicer to generate from markdown or similar (or just a plain straight man page) in the %build phase

issue_title: Useful errors when data within /etc/letsencrypt is inconsistent or corrupt
issue_body:
A recent issue (#2903) showed that if users manually edit the contents of `/etc/letsencrypt` in a way that the client regards as inconsistent (for example, some files no longer exist in the relationships that `storage.py` expects), the client is often not cautious enough to notice and explain the problem, and instead may crash with an exception. This isn't very helpful; what would be more helpful would be to tell the user something like "This certificate lineage appears to have been modified or partially deleted on disk, and the client can't find some of the associated files" instead of (e.g.) "ValueError: max() arg is an empty sequence", or various other cases that it's probably possible to trigger by deleting symlink targets.

We should go through and look at everywhere that the renewer or `storage.py` follows a symlink or creates or reads a file, and make sure that failures based on corrupted lineage structure are handled intelligently.

GettzFunc is a complex class. Adding additional comments which explain the reasons behind the implementation will help first time contributors understand it more quickly. The comments are posted here for convenience and came from the [discussion](https://github.com/dateutil/dateutil/pull/704/files/7e6388418adb8826e7feb057a7d264722b9b3c9c..503b5e9331a4d800bb5875d02612b097c57d12de#diff-b81ed38839c07598f1528a1f038b0460) on PR #704.

> 
> This is not actually the interface. `GettzFunc` is a class representing a callable function. object is the name of the base type of all objects in Python 3 ("new style classes"), and so `GettzFunc` is a subclass of object for backwards compatibility with Python 2.
> 
> I realize it's confusing, but what's being written here is actually the documentation for `tz.gettz`, which is an instance of `GettzFunc`, but for practical purposes is equivalent to a function.
> 
> When you call tz.gettz(), it actually invokes `GettzFunc.__call__`, which itself is a thin wrapper around `GettzFunc.nocache`.
> 
> The reason it's built this way is partially for performance reasons, but mostly because I want `tz.gettz` to satisfy the following invariant:
> 
> `assert tz.gettz(name) is tz.gettz(name)`
> 
> So I need to store a reference to the arguments that have been passed to `tz.gettz` and all the results, so that I can return the same object if I get the same arguments. Those get stored on the `GettzFunc` instance in the `_cache`. Because you might want the old semantics, I've exposed a "nocache" variant that bypasses the cache: `tz.gettz.nocache(name)`, and since `cache` misses are identical to calls to `nocache`, `__call__` is implemented in terms of `nocache`.
> 
> There are also functions on `gettz` for manipulating the cache, like `clear_cache`.

Ping @gioelelm @pl-ki @kasiletti @JobLeonard 

I have been working on a more pythonic and expressive API for attributes in loompy. Currently we access attributes as follows:

```python
ds.col_attrs[“CellID”]   # get a column attribute
ds.row_attrs[“Gene”]   # Get a row attribute

ds.set_attr(“Name”, values, axis=1)   # Set a column attribute
ds.set_attr(“Name”, values, axis=0)   # Set a row attribute

ds.delete_attr(“Name”, axis=1)  # Delete a column attribute
ds.delete_attr(“Name”, axis=0)  # Delete a row attribute
```

We also have convenience access to attributes directly on the LoomConnection object:

```python
ds.Gene  # Happens to be a row attribute
```

But this can collide with object attributes, and row/col attributes can collide with each other.

I want instead to do this::

```python
ds.c.Gene  # Get a column attribute
ds.c[“Gene”]  # Get a column attribute

ds.c.Gene = values  # Set a column attribute
ds.c[“Gene”] = values  # Set a column attribute

del ds.c.Gene  # Delete a column attribute
del ds.c[“Gene”]  # Delete a column attribute
```

And same for rows via `ds.r`

I have written the necessary manager for this. It also **lazy-loads** the values, so nothing is loaded until needed, and it keeps track of the corresponding data in HDF5. It **supports slicing**, so you can slice all the attributes at once (I want to use this for an improved scan() method).

Now, I want to add this to loompy. I can keep `col_attr`,` row_attr`, `set_attr` and `delete_attr` for backwards compatibility. BUT, I can’t keep the direct access attributes (i.e. ds.Gene, ds.Clusters). This will break some code.

Is it ok? If you agree, I will make the change and let you know when you will need to change from `ds.Gene` to `ds.r.Gene` etc. You also can fix your code right now by changing `ds.Gene` to `ds.row_attrs[“Gene”]` if you want.

issue_title: Support methods like NCE, NEG, Hierarchical SoftMax, Blackout or IS
issue_body:
When training a classifier with large number of classes, performance sink. This is expected when using regular (log)SoftMax methods to compute the loss since it needs to normalize current class score with the sum of all other classes score.  

I think this would be helpful to have approximate methods like Hierarchical SoftMax, NCE, NEG, IS to speedup training.

A paper comparing different methods for approximate normalization over all classes:
http://web4.cs.ucl.ac.uk/staff/D.Barber/publications/AISTATS2017.pdf

So far the only thing that's been implemented is a bunch of tools to generate Theano expressions for neural nets. There is no actual training code in the library yet.

We should provide some premade 'training loops' for common use cases, that take care of things like compiling the necessary Theano functions and updating the parameters given a dataset.

It would be great if we could rely on Python generators for this - although at this point I'm not sure if they offer enough flexibility. But if they do, it would be great to be able to avoid adding another class / abstraction.

We could provide a few different types of training loops, for different dataset sizes and approaches. For example, some datasets fit into GPU memory, so we should provide a loop that loads up the data into a shared variable and then iterates over that in batches. But a lot of datasets actually don't, so then we'd have to load a new 'chunk' of data into the shared variable at regular intervals.

Until now I've always reimplemented this type of thing specifically for the problems I was working on (layers.py only provided tools to generate Theano expressions, nothing else). But I've definitely felt like I was reinventing the wheel a couple of times :)

I don't have a concrete idea yet of how we should implement this, input is very welcome.

issue_title: Support cell formatting for item table
issue_body:
Ideally, we want to control cell formatting. This could be how the object is converted to a string (hex, dec) or whether `repr()` or `str()` is used, or how many decimal places are used for display.

This can be split into two sub-problems:

1) How to specify a subset of cells to which a particular formatting rule applies
2) How to specify the formatting rule

Ideas:

The range could be specified with JMESpath specification.

The formatting rule could specify a single-argument python function, though it would be handy to be able to partially apply functions like format(s, '.3f')

We should investigate how to work around this.

issue_title: How to detect or capture the crash?
issue_body:
Could you kindly give me some hint regarding of how to capture the crash when symbolically executing a program within angr?
For example, a statement _raise(SIGSEGV)_ is embed in the program.
When I use angr to symbolically executing the program, however, the execution did not suspend when executing _raise(SIGSEGV)_, and the program can step to the following successors.
I want to capture the crash. How could I do?

Thank you very much.

First of all, let me say that angr is an amazing tool! Thank you for releasing it. I have already started to play with it (e.g., see my pull request in angr-doc https://github.com/angr/angr-doc/pull/75). I have some doubts about Explorer and PathGroup.

The documentation states that:

> Pathgroups are supposed to replace surveyors.Explorer, being more clever and efficient. 

I am observing that this may be not true in some cases. Also, I think that the behavior of Explorer is inconsistent wrt PathGroup, but also wrt to itself. Let me explain why.

1) Explorer can be used like this (A):

```
ex = proj.surveyors.Explorer(..., found=XXX, avoid = YYY, ...)
ex.run() //
```

Or (B): 

```
ex = proj.surveyors.Explorer(..., found=XXX, avoid = YYY, ...)
while True:
   ex.run(1)
   if len(ex.found) >= 1:
        break
```

I was expecting that the result will be same with (A) or (B) but it's not. Explorer is selecting active paths (among active+spilled paths) based on how many times the next instruction of a path have been  executed (see Explorer.path_comparator()). The instruction counter used by path_comparator() is updated: (i) for all states (active + spilled) when Explorer.run() is called, and (ii) for an active state that is explored. This means that if you call many times run() like in (B), you may get a very different instruction counter that in (A) where you call run() only once. Spilled states make the difference.  In my case, on one binary, (B) was able to hit a found path after ~4mins, while (A) did not hit a found path after ~20mins (I stopped it... I don't know if it is able to hit it sooner or later). I don't know when (A) is better of (B), or vice-versa. This was just my experience. I find this behavior a bit confusing or, even a bit inconsistent. I see this as a bug, but this may be a feature.  Where is the truth?

2) Explorer splits active states into: active and spilled. In particular, if you have N active states, max_active states will be "real" active, while N - max_active will be spilled. I am not 100%  sure but I don't see the concept of spilled states in PathGroup and thus all N active states will be explored. In my experiment, the memory usage of PathGroup when used like this:

```
pg = proj.factory.path_group(state) # the same if immutable=False
ex = pg.explore(find = XXX, avoid = YYY)
```

was much higher than Explorer. I had to kill angr when it was using ~4GB, while Explorer used at most ~1.2GB. Explorer is prioritizing (by default!) few active paths based on the instruction counter, while PathGroup is exploring all active states. On my workstation with 8GB of memory, Explorer is more "clever and efficient" than PathGroup. I know that this is may not be true in a general setting, 

My feeling is that PathGroup is much more flexible and powerful than Explorer, but it needs a bit of manual tuning. E.g., avoid PathGroup.explore() but use PathGroup.step() with some user defined "selector_func".

As it is, PathGroup.explore() is not an "equivalent" replacement for Explorer. Again, I am not sure if this  is a design choice or something that may be improved over time.  

Am I missing something about the usage of PathGroup? In particular, how can we "effectively" use it without adding some additional bits?

issue_title: The documentation on readthedoc is not regenerated
issue_body:
Compare for instance
https://pywavelets.readthedocs.org/en/latest/#contributing
and the source.

I noticed that these objects are cached.
```
CurrentMatch: datetime.timedelta(hours=0.5)
FeaturedMatches: datetime.timedelta(hours=0.5)
```
The cache wouldn't know if the match was over, so if you request the currentmatch right before the match actually ends, it will end up caching it for 30min, then the player could get into another match 5 minutes afterwards, thus any subsequent calls to currentmatch would be incorrect.

issue_title: Ensure workers record their own heartbeat records to the database
issue_body:
Issue associated with [story 2519](https://pulp.plan.io/issues/2519).

## Verify that the resource_manager records its own heartbeat

1. Start with a healthy 2.13+ system
2. Stop celerybeat gracefully. On Fedora I do this with `sudo systemctl stop pulp_celerybeat`.
3. Capture the `pulp-admin status` output or the /status/ API
4. Verify that the output from step 3 does NOT show a celerybeat record
5. Verify that the output from step 3 shows at least one resource_manager@ entry. Observe the timestamp for this worker.
6. Verify that the output from step 3 shows at least one reserved_resource_worker-xx@ entry. Observe the timestamp for this worker.
7. Wait 7 seconds to allow another heartbeat to occur and be recorded from both processes. A heartbeat is recorded every 5 seconds so waiting 7 seconds should be adequate.
8. Capture the `pulp-admin status` output or the /status/ API again
9. Verify that the timestamps of any resource_manager@ and reserved_resource_worker-xx@ entries are updated between steps 3 and 8 even though 0 celerybeat@ entries are present

## Verify that the `--heartbeat-interval` is not present on any process

1. Start with a healthy 2.13+ system
2. Verify that no Pulp processes have the `--heartbeat-interval` argument specified. I do this by ensuring this command produces a 0. `ps -awfux | grep celery | grep heartbeat-interval | wc -l`

The API documentation is not listed on Read The Docs right now. This bug was likely introduced by #197. A possible solution is to move `gen_api_docs.sh` to `docs/gen_api_docs.py` (note the file extension change) and ensure the script is called by the appropriate targets in `docs/Makefile`.

This obsoletes #201.

Hi all,

I am using PPO1 as a smaller piece of a larger application - in my scenario, PPO might be called multiple times with different objectives.

I am trying to automatically check for convergence, and in particular, my upstream application requires that I find a stationary point.

One thing I am finding, though, is that for even simple problems, such as pendulum swing-up, the objective value decreases for a while and then oscillates (though the mean reward improves).  A direct correlation between mean reward and objective is not expected due to the simultaneously estimated value function and entropy loss function.  Still, convergence to a stationary point seemingly never occurs.  Even as I increase the number of samples per episode this does not happen, suggesting it is probably not due to sampling variance.

I am wondering:
a) Is there any way to promote convergence?
b) If not, is there a theoretical reason why convergence to a stationary point is so rare or unlikely?

issue_title: Data Request flooding when Operational Dataset changes.
issue_body:
When Operational Dataset changes Leader sends unsolicited Data Response to all neighbor Routers containing new Timestamp value. Every Router in response sends Data Request to get new Dataset. The problem is that all Routers send Data Request at once that causes packet collisions.

The new API is more logical than the old one, and fixes some problems requiring weird client workarounds—notably, the double API calls currently required to send with a template's From address.

Anymail should upgrade. This will be a breaking change for anyone using `esp_extra` with Mailjet.

issue_title: trouble with Listen directives in CentOS 7 / ssl.conf
issue_body:
On a CentOS7 box, I had Listen directives in /etc/httpd/conf.d/ssl.conf binding to a specific IP and port 443:

```
Listen 195.8.117.80:443 https
Listen [2001:67c:1388:1001::6]:443 https
```

and other services running on other IP addresses bound to port 443.

Running letsencrypt-auto I was getting this in the httpd error_log:

```
(98)Address already in use: AH00072: make_sock: could not bind to address [::]:443
(98)Address already in use: AH00072: make_sock: could not bind to address 0.0.0.0:443
```

I stopped the other services temporarily but letsencrypt-auto still failed.

I commented the Listen lines and then the letsencrypt-auto command worked.

After running it, I noticed it had created new Listen directives at the bottom of /etc/httpd/conf/httpd.conf:

```
<IfModule mod_ssl.c>
Listen [2001:67c:1388:1001::6]:443
Listen 195.8.117.80:443
</IfModule>
```

so I didn't uncomment the Listen directives in /etc/httpd/conf.d/ssl.conf

issue_title: Use a single event filter per NettingChannelContract
issue_body:
As suggested by @utzig in #233 we can reduce the number of JSON RPC requests to an ethereum node by using fewer filters.

The easiest solution is to remove the topic filters all together and [filter only by the contract address](https://github.com/ethereum/wiki/wiki/JSON-RPC#eth_newfilter), ignoring the events that cannot be decode (as it is [done in the current master](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/836c3e2a82c76b05460decd36ad8e01e49c403e1/raiden/raiden_service.py#L707-L712))

## Problem Definition

In [ChannelExternalState](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/3d26d3a24175c1dca91d51cbeb26436eb51a0999/raiden/channel/netting_channel.py#L39) in the netting channel we have the `settled_block` property which is set via [`set_settled()`](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/3d26d3a24175c1dca91d51cbeb26436eb51a0999/raiden/channel/netting_channel.py#L91) when `ChannelSettled` event is read.

In the tester we have [ChannelExternalStateTester](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/3d26d3a24175c1dca91d51cbeb26436eb51a0999/raiden/tests/utils/tester_client.py#L120) which should mimick the `ChannelExternalState`. But at the moment it does not as it has no `set_settled()` function and the `settled_block` property is [set to 0](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/3d26d3a24175c1dca91d51cbeb26436eb51a0999/raiden/tests/utils/tester_client.py#L150)

## Solution

Make both `ChannelExternalState` and `ChannelExternalStateTester` have same behaviour around the `set_settled()` function or we may end up getting funny tester errors at some point.

issue_title: Add transport tests for the matrix transport layer
issue_body: Add new fixtures and modify existing tests so that the matrix transport layer can be tested.

### Descritption

At restart we need to catch up with the logs that happened on the blockchain, that means on restart we need to reinstall the filters for all the known token network registries and token networks.

The current implementation relies on the utility methods from the smart contracts to list all the interesting smart contracts and reinstall the filters:

- [Registry.channelManagerAddresses](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/a397d80fe3777f27e0a8d71b6e3548aa05f55701/raiden/smart_contracts/Registry.sol#L71-L88)
- [ChannelManagerContract.getChannelsParticipants](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/master/raiden/smart_contracts/ChannelManagerContract.sol#L83-L118)

The new version of the smart contracts don't have these utility methods, and must use the [available events](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden-contracts/blob/master/raiden_contracts/contracts/TokenNetworkRegistry.sol#L24) to catch up with the smart contract state. For this to work properly, on restart, after the [state has been restored](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/a397d80fe3777f27e0a8d71b6e3548aa05f55701/raiden/raiden_service.py#L240), the [filters must be installed](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/a397d80fe3777f27e0a8d71b6e3548aa05f55701/raiden/raiden_service.py#L276-L279)

### Task list

- [ ] For the known token network registries:
  - [ ] Reinstall the filters using the last confirmed `from_block` from the previous run 
- [ ] For the known token networks:
  - [ ] Reinstall the filters using the last confirmed `from_block` from the previous run
- [ ] For the new token networks, created while the node was offline:
  - [ ] Install the filters either using the last confirmed `from_block` from the previous run, or the block number at which the new network was deployed

issue_title: Add on-chain secret reveal if a secret is about to expire
issue_body:
Integrate the `SecretRegistry` contract and reveal secrets on-chain if they are about to expire

- [x] add `events_for_onchain_secretregister` - `transfer/mediated_transfer/target.py`
- [ ] add events for triggering a `ContractSendSecretReveal` also in `transfer/mediated_transfer/mediator.py`
- [x] add `ContractSendSecretReveal`
- [x] add `handle_contract_secretreveal` in `raiden_event_handler.py` and register the secret
- [x] add unit test

## Problem Definition

Inducing a channel event to be fired (for example, by depositing to the channel) causes the event to appear on channel events API. But, after a small time (some seconds), fetching this same event, without `from_block` parameter, returns empty list. Looks like Raiden is _forgeting_ past events.

## Example

Deposit 3 tokens to the channel:
```
$ curl 'http://localhost:5001/api/1/channels/0xd54195db14a12b79ae33b23fdc75df1ebe79d277' -X PATCH -H 'Content-Type: application/json' --data-binary '{"balance":3}'
{
  "balance": 10, 
  "channel_address": "0xd54195db14a12b79ae33b23fdc75df1ebe79d277", 
  "partner_address": "0x000d91cf263a11f9bfcee3752e5b03fc1196ce98", 
  "settle_timeout": 600, 
  "state": "opened", 
  "token_address": "0x9aba529db3ff2d8409a1da4c9eb148879b046700"
}
```
Check events:
```
$ curl http://localhost:5001/api/1/events/channels/0xd54195db14a12b79ae33b23fdc75df1ebe79d277
[{"block_number": 0, "participant": "b0d5eeaeb7b084a2ba92c24b79f4f2d079a3062b", "event_type": "ChannelNewBalance", "balance": 24, "token_address": "9aba529db3ff2d8409a1da4c9eb148879b046700"}]
```
Check events again after 30s:
```
$ curl http://localhost:5001/api/1/events/channels/0xd54195db14a12b79ae33b23fdc75df1ebe79d277
[]
```

**Another** issue can be seen in this example: as seen in the `PATCH` request, channel `balance` isn't being updated, and following requests to `/api/1/channels` also return old balance.

## Solution

Make sure the events are being persisted and remembered, at least during session, but preferably, across sessions. Also, guarantee channel balances are being correctly updated.

## System Description
Master 1d6633dbb9ef343675fc52950f2aee85d97504a9 , from today.

issue_title: CLI: Memory defaults of "zhmc partition create" do not work
issue_labels: [{'id': 429586299, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/zhmcclient/python-zhmcclient/labels/area:%20code', 'name': 'area: code', 'color': '207de5', 'default': False}, {'id': 429588767, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/zhmcclient/python-zhmcclient/labels/resolution:%20fixed', 'name': 'resolution: fixed', 'color': 'cccccc', 'default': False}, {'id': 429587708, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/zhmcclient/python-zhmcclient/labels/type:%20bug', 'name': 'type: bug', 'color': '84b6eb', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
### Actual behavior

The command:

    zhmc partition create <cec-name> --name <part-name> --ifl-processors 4

fails with:

    Error: HTTPError: 400,5: Required field(s) 'initial-memory', 'maximum-memory', missing
    from object definition.
    [POST /api/cpcs/fa1f2466-12df-311a-804c-4ed2cc1d6564/partitions]

The zhmc help text suggests that `initial-memory` and `maximum-memory` each have a default of 1024 MB.

Logging the partition creation with `hmc=debug` reveals that the request body of the POST method that creates the partition indeed does not have any of these properties:

    DEBUG zhmcclient.hmc: HMC request: POST https://9.152.151.49:6794/api/cpcs/fa1f2466-12df-311a-804c-4ed2cc1d6564/partitions,
    headers: {
        'X-API-Session': ...,
        'Content-type': 'application/json',
        'Accept': '*/*'
    },
    content(max.1000): '{"ifl-processors": 4, "name": "simon_vnics"}'

    DEBUG zhmcclient.hmc: HMC response: POST https://9.152.151.49:6794/api/cpcs/fa1f2466-12df-311a-804c-4ed2cc1d6564/partitions,
    status: 400,
    headers: {
        'x-request-id': ...,
        'Content-Length': '549',
        'Server': 'zSeries management console API web server / 2.0',
        'Connection': 'close',
        'Cache-control': 'no-cache',
        'Date': 'Wed, 19 Apr 2017 09:10:02 GMT',
        'Content-Type': 'application/json'},
    content(max.1000): '{"http-status":400,"reason":5,"request-uri":"/api/cpcs/fa1f2466-12df-311a-804c-4ed2cc1d6564/partitions","request-method":"POST","message":"Required field(s) \'initial-memory\', \'maximum-memory\', missing from object definition.","request-headers":{"content-type":"application/json","connection":"keep-alive","host":"9.152.151.49:6794","content-length":"44","accept":"*/*","x-api-session":"...","user-agent":"python-requests/2.13.0","accept-encoding":"gzip, deflate"},"request-authenticated-as":"ensadmin"}'

### Expected behavior

The stated defaults for `initial-memory` and `maximum-memory`should be applied when the respective options are not specified.

For that to happen, the CLI layer needs to add them to the list of input properties for the create partition method.

### Execution environment

* zhmcclient version: 0.12.0.dev3
* Operating system (type+version): RHEL 7.2

issue_title: Damaged session if direct_tcpip channel open fails
issue_body:
```
import socket
from ssh2.session import Session
from ssh2.listener import *

username='user'
passwd='password'
serverA='myServerA'
serverB='doesNotExist'

#open socket connection
sock = socket.socket(socket.IF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
sock.connect((serverA, 22))

#open session and authenticate
session = Session()
session.handshake(sock)
session.userauth_password(username, passwd)
session.set_timeout(2000)

#demonstrate successful channel open and execute command on serverA
chan = session.open_session()
if chan:
    print('successfully opened channel with serverA')
    chan.execute('ls')
    chan.close()
else:
    print('Failed to open channel with serverA')

#Try to open direct tcpip channel, via serverA SSH tunnel, to unreachable serverB
direct_chan = session.direct_tcpip(serverB, 22)
if direct_chan:
    print('ServerB was reached')
else:
    print('ServerB was unreachable')

#Cannot now open channel with serverA - why???
chan = session.open_session()
if chan:
    print('successfully opened channel with serverA')
    chan.execute('ls')
    chan.close()
else:
    print('Failed to open channel with serverA')

session.disconnect()
sock.close()
```

__Expected behaviour:__ 
Assume serverA is a reachable SSH server, and serverB is unreachable.  You successfully open a channel and execute a command on serverA.  With the same session, you try to open a tunneled direct_tcpip connection to an unreachable serverB.  The direct_tcpip channel fails as expected.  I would then still expect to be able to use the same session to sucessfully open a channel and execute a command  on serverA a second time

__Actual behaviour:__ 
The second time you try to open a channel to serverA to execute a command, the open_session fails.  It's like the session has been damaged by the failure to open the direct_tcpip connection to serverB. 

Note that if serverB is reachable, then the session is still ok, and you can still open a channel to serverA and execute a command.

Why does this happen and is there a fix?  I want to continue using a session for communication with serverA even if serverB is unreachable.

Annotations now have a somewhat "official" syntax in python, courtesy of https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0484/. I propose we deprecate the annotation syntax for interacts.

The PEP does say:

> Note that this PEP still explicitly does NOT prevent other uses of annotations, nor does it require (or forbid) any particular processing of annotations, even when they conform to this specification. It simply enables better coordination, as PEP 333 did for web frameworks.

However, I think the annotation syntax doesn't add enough value and over our decorator-based syntax and is not widespread enough to maintain it.

@SylvainCorlay, @ellisonbg, and others: thoughts?

@jasongrout @SylvainCorlay 
Since buffers can be part of the state, we need to think how they will be stored in the json for embedding. 
For the js and the py side we can simply use the same code we use to pluck out and insert the buffers, it's only a matter of how to store it in the json state.
I suggest sth like this (looking at the [schema](https://github.com/jupyter-widgets/ipywidgets/blob/master/jupyter-widgets-schema/v1/state.schema.json)), by example:

```
...
state: {
  description: ...
  buffers:  [
   [['MODEL...', 'bla', 1], {'encoding: ['base64'], data: 'AF... 33'}],
   [['MODEL...., 'lala', 'x'], {'encoding: [], url: 'MODEL_123_lala_x.dat'}]
 ]
}
```
so it's a list with [path, data object] pairs, where data object specified how it is encoded (as a list, so it can be first zipped, then base64'ed). Or it could be a url, this would allow to load binary files efficiently, since a webserver can zip it on the fly. The only issue would be, can you from a ajax request get a Buffer object?

issue_title: Universal way of waiting for file descriptors
issue_body:
I'm writing an async wrapper over psycopg2 for my ORM to work with trio, and I've hit a snag with it - as far as I can see in the docs, there's no universal way of waiting until a file descriptor is readable.

There's `trio.hazmat.wait_readable`, but that only works on *nix, and `wait_socket_readable` (as shown in the docs) only works on `socket.socket` objects. 

I took a quick search of the issues and couldn't find anything related to this either.

Given `pathlib.Path.iterdir` returns a generator that does IO access on each iteration, `trio.Path.iterdir` is currently broken given it currently only generates the generator asynchronously (which I suppose is pointless given there is no need for IO at generator creation)

The solution would be to modify  `trio.Path.iterdir` to return an async generator, however this means creating a special case given the current implementation is only an async wrapper on `pathlib.Path.iterdir`.

The documentation says:

> This method may block in order to perform a “graceful” shutdown. [..] This requires sending a “goodbye” message; 

And the implementation is:

```
    async def aclose(self):
        self.socket.close()
        await _core.checkpoint()
```

For a "graceful close" of a TCP connection I would at the very least expect a call to `socket.shutdown()` first. Ideally, it would behave as https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7230#section-6.6 prescribes for HTTP:

>    If a server performs an immediate close of a TCP connection, there is
>    a significant risk that the client will not be able to read the last
>    HTTP response.  If the server receives additional data from the
>    client on a fully closed connection, such as another request that was
>    sent by the client before receiving the server's response, the
>    server's TCP stack will send a reset packet to the client;
>    unfortunately, the reset packet might erase the client's
>    unacknowledged input buffers before they can be read and interpreted
>    by the client's HTTP parser.
> 
>    To avoid the TCP reset problem, servers typically close a connection
>    in stages.  First, the server performs a half-close by closing only
>    the write side of the read/write connection.  The server then
>    continues to read from the connection until it receives a
>    corresponding close by the client, or until the server is reasonably
>    certain that its own TCP stack has received the client's
>    acknowledgement of the packet(s) containing the server's last
>    response.  Finally, the server fully closes the connection.

Thoughts?

issue_title: It's very annoying that fixtures can't yield inside a nursery/cancel scope
issue_body:
Right now, writing code like this is broken:

```python
@pytest.fixture
async def background_task_fixture():
    async with trio.open_nursery() as nursery:
        await nursery.start(...)
        yield
        nursery.cancel_scope.cancel()
```

The problem is that the cancel scope semantics assume that they're strictly nested and can "see" all exceptions that happen inside them, but the convention for pytest fixtures is that we never throw in any exceptions. Also, right now we setup and teardown fixtures in a strict sequential order, but it might be nice to someday do that concurrently, and if we do then this example would *really* explode (you'll get assertion errors inside `trio/_core/_run.py`). (Previous discussion: https://github.com/python-trio/pytest-trio/issues/25#issuecomment-352990630)

For this particular case, that's not a big deal – you can write it as 

```python
@pytest.fixture
async def background_task_fixture(test_nursery):
    await test_nursery.start(...)
```

and that's actually better than the original.

But, as @touilleMan [points out](https://github.com/python-trio/pytest-trio/pull/47#discussion_r204700827), there are other cases where this is really quite a problem. Specifically, cases where the use of a nursery is abstracted away inside some other context manager. For example, consider these two fixtures:

```python
@pytest.fixture
async def tcp_client():
    async with await open_tcp_stream(...) as stream:
        yield stream

@pytest.fixture
async def websocket_client():
    async with open_websocket(...) as websocket:
        yield websocket
```

The first one is fine; the second one uses a nursery internally, so it's broken. But to realize that, we have to know what's going on internally in some random third-party library, and that's pretty terrible. So... what can we do to make this better?
[more-itertools](https://pypi.org/project/more-itertools/) is required to run ArchivesSnake, and must be installed prior to running the example script
It's just an extra vocab word that needs to be mentally mapped by users.
